text: The surgeon places incisions in a vertical line along the left rectus muscle, preparing the surgical field for further intervention.
The anatomy becomes visible, showing the sigmoid colon tethered to the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon gradually dissects the tissue, revealing the terminal ileum as it enters the cecum, with visible anatomical structures becoming clearer.
The surgeon observes the coalescence of the tinea, which visually indicates the likely location of the base of the appendix.
A small fissure in the inflamed tissue is identified as the body of the appendix, visible amidst the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to continue dissection, separating the ligament of Treves from the body of the appendix.
The surgeon carefully elevates the appendix from the surrounding inflammatory tissue, revealing its position relative to adjacent structures.
The surgeon uses curved monopolar scissors to dissect the remaining portion of the appendix, with the anatomy now clearly visible.
The surgeon creates a space between the appendix and the mesoappendix, carefully separating these structures to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the mesoappendix within the tissue on the left side of the surgical field, distinguishing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to cauterize the appendiceal artery, ensuring hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon uses curved monopolar scissors to transect the appendix, separating it from the surrounding inflammatory tissue.
The surgeon encounters some bleeding while dissecting the appendix, but quickly controls it using appropriate hemostatic techniques.
The robotic console's wrists allow precise manipulation at an angle within the dissected cavity, enhancing access to the recess created between the appendix and the mesoappendix.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate the remaining base of the appendix from the cecum, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon ties three interrupted sutures around the base of the appendix using an absorbable suture, securing the structure following its dissection from the cecum.
The surgeon places two simple sutures at the base of the appendix without imbricating the stump, leaving it exposed.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical field to remove clots and flushes the dependent portions of the pelvis, ensuring a clear view of the area.
The surgeon deploys the endo-catch bag from the superior port site, which may require dilation. This site is chosen due to its lower risk for future incisional hernias.
The surgeon introduces a drain through the inferior most port site, positioning it within the surgical field.
The surgical drape is positioned over the pelvis and the surgical field, covering the area where the procedure is being performed.
The surgeon closes the dilated extraction port at the fascia using a vicryl suture, securing the tissue layers.
